gu-embryology-primary.name: Patient
gu-embryology-primary.variantDescription: Mesonephric vs paramesonephric duct development, sexual differentiation, common anomalies (hypospadias, cryptorchidism, inguinal hernia)
keyFindings: Wolffian (mesonephric) duct → male internal genitalia (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles)
Müllerian (paramesonephric) duct → female internal genitalia (fallopian tubes, uterus, upper vagina)
SRY gene → testis determining factor → testosterone + AMH
AMH causes Müllerian duct regression
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of GU Embryology

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Wolffian (mesonephric) duct → male internal genitalia (epididymis, vas deferens, seminal vesicles)
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

renal-embryology-primary.name: Patient
renal-embryology-primary.variantDescription: Pronephros → mesonephros → metanephros development, ureteric bud and metanephric mesenchyme interaction, renal anomalies
keyFindings: Ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct) → collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter)
Metanephric mesenchyme → nephrons (glomerulus through DCT)
Reciprocal induction between ureteric bud and mesenchyme
Common anomalies: horseshoe kidney, pelvic kidney, duplex collecting system, renal agenesis
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Renal Embryology

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Ureteric bud (from mesonephric duct) → collecting system (collecting ducts, calyces, renal pelvis, ureter)
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

msk-embryology-primary.name: Patient
msk-embryology-primary.variantDescription: Somite differentiation (sclerotome, myotome, dermatome), limb development, bone formation (endochondral vs intramembranous)
keyFindings: Somites → sclerotome (vertebrae/ribs), myotome (skeletal muscle), dermatome (dermis)
Limb buds: AER (apical ectodermal ridge) → proximal-distal growth
ZPA (zone of polarizing activity) → anterior-posterior patterning (SHH gene)
Endochondral ossification: cartilage template → bone (long bones)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of MSK Embryology

endocrine-embryology-primary.name: Patient
endocrine-embryology-primary.chiefComplaint: 2-year-old with painless midline neck mass that moves with swallowing
endocrine-embryology-primary.variantDescription: Thyroid, parathyroid, adrenal, and pituitary gland development; pharyngeal pouch derivatives; common congenital anomalies
keyFindings: Thyroid: foramen cecum → descends via thyroglossal duct → normal position at C5-T1
Thyroglossal duct cyst: most common midline neck mass in children, moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion
Parathyroid: 3rd pouch (inferior) and 4th pouch (superior) — inferior parathyroids migrate FURTHER
Adrenal cortex: mesoderm; Adrenal medulla: neural crest cells
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Endocrine Embryology

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Thyroid: foramen cecum → descends via thyroglossal duct → normal position at C5-T1

gi-embryology-primary.name: Patient
gi-embryology-primary.chiefComplaint: Neonate with bilious vomiting on day 1 of life
gi-embryology-primary.variantDescription: Foregut, midgut, hindgut derivatives; midgut rotation; vitelline duct; common GI malformations
keyFindings: Foregut: esophagus to proximal duodenum (to ampulla of Vater), liver, gallbladder, pancreas
Midgut: distal duodenum to proximal 2/3 transverse colon — supplied by SMA
Hindgut: distal 1/3 transverse colon to upper rectum — supplied by IMA
Midgut rotation: 270° counterclockwise around SMA; malrotation → Ladd bands → volvulus
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of GI Embryology

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Foregut: esophagus to proximal duodenum (to ampulla of Vater), liver, gallbladder, pancreas

pharyngeal-apparatus-primary.name: Patient
pharyngeal-apparatus-primary.chiefComplaint: Newborn with preauricular pit and branchial cleft cyst
pharyngeal-apparatus-primary.variantDescription: Pharyngeal arches, pouches, clefts, and membrane derivatives; cranial nerve associations; common anomalies
keyFindings: 1st arch: mandible, maxilla, malleus, incus (CN V)
2nd arch: stapes, styloid, lesser horn of hyoid (CN VII)
3rd arch: greater horn of hyoid, stylopharyngeus (CN IX)
4th/6th arches: laryngeal cartilages (CN X — SLN and RLN)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Pharyngeal Apparatus

jejunal-ileal-atresia-primary.name: Patient
jejunal-ileal-atresia-primary.chiefComplaint: Neonate with bilious vomiting and abdominal distension
jejunal-ileal-atresia-primary.variantDescription: Vascular accident theory, apple-peel atresia, triple bubble sign, surgical management
keyFindings: Caused by in-utero mesenteric vascular accident (NOT failed recanalization like duodenal atresia)
Types: I (membrane), II (fibrous cord), IIIa (V-shaped mesentery gap), IIIb (apple-peel/Christmas tree), IV (multiple)
X-ray: dilated proximal loops, air-fluid levels, no distal gas
Contrast enema: microcolon (unused)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Jejunal/Ileal Atresia

cdh-congenital-diaphragmatic-hernia-prim.name: Patient
cdh-congenital-diaphragmatic-hernia-prim.chiefComplaint: Newborn with respiratory distress and absent left breath sounds
cdh-congenital-diaphragmatic-hernia-prim.variantDescription: Bochdalek vs Morgagni hernia, pulmonary hypoplasia, persistent pulmonary hypertension
keyFindings: Bochdalek (posterolateral, LEFT 85%) — most common; Morgagni (anterior, right)
Failure of pleuroperitoneal membrane closure at 8-10 weeks
Abdominal contents herniate into thorax → pulmonary hypoplasia + persistent pulmonary HTN
Do NOT bag-mask ventilate (distends stomach in thorax)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Congenital Diaphragmatic Hernia

conotruncal-defects-primary.name: Patient
conotruncal-defects-primary.chiefComplaint: Cyanotic newborn unresponsive to supplemental oxygen
conotruncal-defects-primary.variantDescription: Neural crest cell migration, truncal septation, Tetralogy of Fallot, transposition, truncus arteriosus, 22q11 deletion
keyFindings: Neural crest cells form the aorticopulmonary septum (divides truncus into aorta and pulmonary artery)
Failed spiral septation → Transposition of Great Arteries (parallel circulations)
Unequal septation → Tetralogy of Fallot (anterior deviation of infundibular septum)
No septation → Persistent Truncus Arteriosus
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Conotruncal Defects

gi-embryology-malrotation-primary.name: Patient
gi-embryology-malrotation-primary.variantDescription: Normal midgut rotation, Ladd bands, midgut volvulus, upper GI series diagnosis
keyFindings: Normal: 270° counterclockwise rotation around SMA
Malrotation: incomplete rotation → cecum in RUQ, duodenojejunal junction displaced
Ladd bands: peritoneal bands crossing duodenum (cause obstruction)
Volvulus: midgut twists around SMA → ischemia → necrosis (surgical EMERGENCY)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Intestinal Malrotation & Volvulus

hirschsprung-disease-primary.name: Patient
hirschsprung-disease-primary.chiefComplaint: Neonate who has not passed meconium at 48 hours
hirschsprung-disease-primary.variantDescription: Neural crest cell migration failure, aganglionic bowel, transition zone, RET gene, suction rectal biopsy
keyFindings: Failed migration of neural crest cells → absent ganglion cells in Meissner and Auerbach plexuses
Always involves rectum, extends proximally (short-segment most common)
Functional obstruction: aganglionic segment stays contracted
Diagnosis: suction rectal biopsy (absent ganglion cells, hypertrophied nerve fibers)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Hirschsprung Disease

horseshoe-kidney-primary.name: Patient
horseshoe-kidney-primary.chiefComplaint: Horseshoe kidney found on abdominal CT for unrelated reason
horseshoe-kidney-primary.variantDescription: Fusion of lower poles, inferior mesenteric artery entrapment, Turner syndrome association
keyFindings: Lower poles fuse across midline during ascent from pelvis
Gets trapped under inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) — cannot ascend to normal position
Usually asymptomatic, found incidentally
Complications: UPJ obstruction, stones, UTIs, increased Wilms tumor risk
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Horseshoe Kidney

meckel-diverticulum-primary.name: Patient
meckel-diverticulum-primary.chiefComplaint: 2-year-old with painless bright red rectal bleeding
meckel-diverticulum-primary.variantDescription: Vitelline duct remnant, rule of 2s, ectopic gastric mucosa, Meckel scan, painless rectal bleeding
keyFindings: Remnant of vitelline (omphalomesenteric) duct
Rule of 2s: 2% prevalence, 2 feet from ileocecal valve, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric/pancreatic), presents by age 2
Most common cause of painless lower GI bleeding in children <2 years
Ectopic gastric mucosa → acid secretion → ulceration → bleeding
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Meckel Diverticulum

omphalocele-gastroschisis-primary.name: Patient
omphalocele-gastroschisis-primary.chiefComplaint: Prenatal ultrasound showing abdominal wall defect with bowel herniation
omphalocele-gastroschisis-primary.variantDescription: Lateral fold closure defects vs vascular disruption, peritoneal covering difference, associated anomalies
keyFindings: Omphalocele: midline, covered by peritoneal sac, contains bowel ± liver, many associated anomalies (trisomies, Beckwith-Wiedemann)
Gastroschisis: right of umbilicus, NO peritoneal covering, bowel directly exposed, few associated anomalies
Omphalocele: failure of lateral body folds to close
Gastroschisis: likely vascular disruption of right omphalomesenteric artery
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Omphalocele & Gastroschisis

septal-defects-primary.name: Patient
septal-defects-primary.chiefComplaint: 6-month-old with heart murmur and failure to thrive
septal-defects-primary.variantDescription: Septum primum/secundum formation, endocardial cushion defects, VSD types, hemodynamic consequences
keyFindings: ASD: Ostium secundum (most common — defect in fossa ovalis area), Ostium primum (endocardial cushion — associated with Down syndrome)
VSD: Most common congenital heart defect; Membranous (most common type), Muscular
Small VSD: loud holosystolic murmur, often closes spontaneously
Large VSD: heart failure, failure to thrive, pulmonary overcirculation

tef-esophageal-atresia-primary.name: Patient
tef-esophageal-atresia-primary.chiefComplaint: Newborn with excessive drooling and choking with first feed
tef-esophageal-atresia-primary.variantDescription: Five types of TEF/EA, VACTERL association, diagnosis by inability to pass NG tube, H-type fistula
keyFindings: Most common (85%): EA with distal TEF (Type C) — blind proximal esophageal pouch + fistula from distal esophagus to trachea
Polyhydramnios prenatally (cannot swallow amniotic fluid)
Diagnosis: inability to pass nasogastric tube (coils in proximal pouch on X-ray)
Gas in stomach with EA = distal fistula present; Gasless abdomen = pure EA without fistula
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of TEF & Esophageal Atresia

mcqs.question: What is the most important consideration in this case?

options.text: Most common (85%): EA with distal TEF (Type C) — blind proximal esophageal pouch + fistula from distal esophagus to trachea
options.explanation: This is the key concept.
Further evaluation is warranted.

thyroglossal-branchial-primary.name: Patient
thyroglossal-branchial-primary.variantDescription: Thyroglossal duct cyst vs branchial cleft cyst, location and movement characteristics, Sistrunk procedure
keyFindings: Thyroglossal duct cyst: MIDLINE, moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion, most common congenital midline neck mass in children
Branchial cleft cyst: LATERAL (anterior to SCM), most are 2nd cleft (angle of mandible)
Thyroglossal cyst treatment: Sistrunk procedure (excise cyst + central hyoid bone + tract to foramen cecum)
Must confirm normal thyroid tissue exists before excision (may be only thyroid tissue)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Thyroglossal & Branchial Cleft Anomalies

options.text: Thyroglossal duct cyst: MIDLINE, moves with swallowing AND tongue protrusion, most common congenital midline neck mass in children

neuronal-migration-primary.name: Patient
neuronal-migration-primary.chiefComplaint: Infant with refractory seizures and developmental delay
neuronal-migration-primary.variantDescription: Lissencephaly, polymicrogyria, heterotopia, schizencephaly; timing and mechanisms
keyFindings: Neuronal migration occurs weeks 12-24 of gestation
Lissencephaly (smooth brain): failed migration → absent gyri (agyria) or reduced gyri (pachygyria); LIS1, DCX genes
Polymicrogyria: excessive small gyri from late migration/organization defect
Heterotopia: neurons stop before reaching cortex (periventricular nodular heterotopia — seizures, normal intelligence)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Neuronal Migration Disorders

congenital-hydrocephalus-primary.name: Patient
congenital-hydrocephalus-primary.variantDescription: Aqueductal stenosis, communicating vs non-communicating, macrocephaly, VP shunt
keyFindings: Most common cause: aqueductal stenosis (obstruction of cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius)
Non-communicating (obstructive): blockage within ventricular system
Communicating: impaired CSF absorption at arachnoid granulations
Signs: macrocephaly, bulging fontanelle, sunset eyes (upgaze palsy), irritability
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Congenital Hydrocephalus

corpus-callosum-agenesis-primary.name: Patient
corpus-callosum-agenesis-primary.chiefComplaint: Fetal MRI showing absent corpus callosum
corpus-callosum-agenesis-primary.variantDescription: Development of commissural fibers, complete vs partial agenesis, Probst bundles, associated syndromes
keyFindings: Corpus callosum develops anterior to posterior (genu → body → splenium → rostrum)
Partial agenesis affects posterior portions (splenium) since they form last
Probst bundles: longitudinal callosal fibers that fail to cross (run parallel instead)
Wide range of outcomes: normal intelligence to severe disability depending on associated anomalies
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Agenesis of Corpus Callosum

dandy-walker-primary.name: Patient
dandy-walker-primary.chiefComplaint: Infant with macrocephaly and cerebellar signs
dandy-walker-primary.variantDescription: Posterior fossa abnormalities, cerebellar vermis hypoplasia, hydrocephalus association
keyFindings: Classic triad: cystic dilation of 4th ventricle + complete/partial absence of cerebellar vermis + enlarged posterior fossa
Hydrocephalus present in 80% of cases
Dandy-Walker variant: less severe, partial vermis hypoplasia, less posterior fossa enlargement
Associated with chromosomal anomalies, other brain malformations
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Dandy-Walker Malformation

holoprosencephaly-primary.name: Patient
holoprosencephaly-primary.chiefComplaint: Newborn with cyclopia and single nostril
holoprosencephaly-primary.variantDescription: Failure of prosencephalon to cleave, spectrum (alobar to lobar), SHH gene, midline facial defects
keyFindings: Failure of prosencephalon (forebrain) to divide into two hemispheres
Spectrum: alobar (most severe, single ventricle) → semilobar → lobar (mildest)
Midline facial defects: cyclopia, proboscis, cleft lip/palate, hypotelorism
'The face predicts the brain' — facial severity correlates with brain malformation
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Holoprosencephaly

neural-tube-defects-primary.name: Patient
neural-tube-defects-primary.chiefComplaint: Prenatal screening showing elevated maternal serum AFP
neural-tube-defects-primary.variantDescription: Primary neurulation, failure of closure, anencephaly, spina bifida spectrum, folate prevention
keyFindings: Neural tube closes by day 28 (anterior neuropore day 25, posterior neuropore day 28)
Anencephaly: failure of anterior neuropore closure → absent cerebral hemispheres (lethal)
Spina bifida occulta: vertebral arch defect only, skin-covered, usually asymptomatic (tuft of hair, dimple)
Meningocele: meninges herniate through defect (good prognosis)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Neural Tube Defects

congenital-lobar-emphysema-primary.name: Patient
congenital-lobar-emphysema-primary.chiefComplaint: Infant with progressive respiratory distress and hyperinflated left upper lobe on CXR
congenital-lobar-emphysema-primary.variantDescription: Hyperinflation of a lobe, cartilage deficiency, progressive respiratory distress, lobectomy
keyFindings: Hyperinflation of one or more lobes due to cartilage deficiency or external compression
Most common: left upper lobe > right middle lobe > right upper lobe
Ball-valve mechanism: air enters but cannot escape
CXR: hyperlucent lobe with mediastinal shift, compression of adjacent lung
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Congenital Lobar Emphysema

laryngotracheal-development-primary.name: Patient
laryngotracheal-development-primary.chiefComplaint: Neonate with inspiratory stridor worsening with feeding
laryngotracheal-development-primary.variantDescription: Laryngotracheal groove, respiratory diverticulum, tracheoesophageal septum, laryngeal anomalies
keyFindings: Laryngotracheal groove appears in floor of primitive pharynx at week 4
Respiratory diverticulum grows ventrally, separated from foregut by tracheoesophageal septum
Incomplete septum → tracheoesophageal fistula
Laryngomalacia: most common cause of stridor in infants (floppy epiglottis/arytenoids)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Laryngotracheal Development

cpam-bronchogenic-cyst-primary.variantDescription: Congenital pulmonary airway malformation types, bronchogenic cyst pathogenesis and location
keyFindings: CPAM (formerly CCAM): hamartomatous overgrowth of terminal bronchioles
Type 1 (most common, best prognosis): large cysts >2cm
Type 2: small cysts <2cm, associated with other anomalies
Type 3 (worst prognosis): microcystic/solid, large lesions

pulmonary-sequestration-primary.name: Patient
pulmonary-sequestration-primary.chiefComplaint: Child with recurrent left lower lobe pneumonia
pulmonary-sequestration-primary.variantDescription: Intralobar vs extralobar, systemic arterial supply, recurrent infections
keyFindings: Non-functioning lung tissue with SYSTEMIC arterial supply (not pulmonary)
Intralobar (75%): within visceral pleura, no own pleural covering, venous drainage to pulmonary veins
Extralobar (25%): own pleural covering, venous drainage to systemic veins (azygos/IVC), associated with CDH
Both most common in left lower lobe
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Pulmonary Sequestration

surfactant-deficiency-rds-primary.name: Patient
surfactant-deficiency-rds-primary.chiefComplaint: 32-week premature infant with progressive respiratory distress
surfactant-deficiency-rds-primary.variantDescription: Type II pneumocyte development, surfactant composition, respiratory distress syndrome of newborn, lecithin-sphingomyelin ratio
keyFindings: Surfactant produced by Type II pneumocytes starting ~24 weeks, adequate by ~35 weeks
Surfactant = DPPC (dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine) + surfactant proteins (SP-A, B, C, D)
Reduces alveolar surface tension → prevents atelectasis
RDS: ground-glass opacities + air bronchograms on CXR
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Surfactant Deficiency & RDS

skeletal-dysplasia-primary.name: Patient
skeletal-dysplasia-primary.chiefComplaint: Newborn with short limbs, normal trunk, and macrocephaly
skeletal-dysplasia-primary.variantDescription: Endochondral vs intramembranous ossification, achondroplasia (FGFR3), osteogenesis imperfecta (collagen I), thanatophoric dysplasia
keyFindings: Achondroplasia: FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation → impaired endochondral ossification → rhizomelic (proximal) limb shortening
Osteogenesis imperfecta: Type I collagen defect → brittle bones, blue sclerae, hearing loss
Thanatophoric dysplasia: lethal, FGFR3 mutation, cloverleaf skull
Endochondral: cartilage → bone (long bones, vertebrae, pelvis)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Skeletal Dysplasia & Bone Formation

options.text: Achondroplasia: FGFR3 gain-of-function mutation → impaired endochondral ossification → rhizomelic (proximal) limb shortening

Further evaluation is warranted.

Asymmetric, non-pattern distribution (unlike genetic syndromes)
Spectrum: constriction rings → lymphedema → amputation of digits/limbs
Craniofacial clefts that DON'T follow standard cleft lip/palate lines

polydactyly-syndactyly-primary.name: Patient
polydactyly-syndactyly-primary.chiefComplaint: Newborn with extra digit on ulnar side of hand
polydactyly-syndactyly-primary.variantDescription: Digital ray formation, apoptosis in web spaces, preaxial vs postaxial polydactyly associations
keyFindings: Digital rays form from limb bud mesenchyme; programmed cell death (apoptosis) separates digits
Syndactyly: failure of apoptosis between digital rays (cutaneous vs osseous)
Polydactyly: extra digits; Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) most common, often isolated/autosomal dominant in Black populations
Preaxial polydactyly (radial/tibial): more likely associated with syndromes (Holt-Oram, Fanconi)
commonPitfalls: Not recognizing key features of Polydactyly & Syndactyly
